findings.evidence_quote: TurboTax Refund Advance is a loan based upon your anticipated refund and is not the refund itself. 0% APR and $0 loan fees.
findings.location: Loan details and disclosures for the TurboTax Refund Advance program footnote
findings.reasoning: Trigger: a rate of finance charge is stated for the Refund Advance loan product. Requirement: the rate is expressed as '0% APR', satisfying the rule that any stated finance charge rate must be expressed as an APR. Axis A compliant. No specific library entry governs APR phrasing generically, so axis B is na. Counter-reading considered: since it's 0%, one might argue no 'finance charge' is truly being charged so the rule doesn't trigger; however the rate is still explicitly stated and correctly labeled as APR, so treating it as triggered-and-compliant is the more faithful reading, consistent with the worked example.

findings.evidence_quote: File Now, Pay Later is available with a 3, 6, or 9 month loan term. Variable APR ranging from 15%-33%. For example, a 6-month $2,000 loan with an APR of 19% has a finance charge of $112.28 and 6 monthly installments of $352.05 each.
findings.location: Loan details and disclosures for the File Now, Pay Later program footnote
findings.reasoning: Trigger: a rate of finance charge is stated for the File Now, Pay Later loan ('Variable APR ranging from 15%-33%', 'APR of 19%'). Requirement: the rate is expressly stated as an APR (Annual Percentage Rate), and the example even ties the APR to a computed finance charge dollar amount ($112.28), demonstrating proper APR-based disclosure. Axis A compliant. No library entry specifically governs this generic APR-labeling requirement beyond the rule itself, so axis B is na. Counter-reading considered: one might argue the disclosure should also independently label the $112.28 as a 'finance charge' disclosure separate from APR to fully satisfy Reg Z-style disclosures, but the rule as scoped here only asks whether the rate was stated as an APR, which it clearly was.

findings.evidence_quote: TurboTax Refund Advance is a loan based upon your anticipated refund and is not the refund itself. 0% APR and $0 loan fees.
findings.location: TurboTax Refund Advance loan details and disclosures section
findings.reasoning: Rule R-02 triggers when any rate of finance charge is stated. On this page, there are loan products mentioned with finance charge information. In the TurboTax Refund Advance disclosure, the rate is explicitly stated as "0% APR," which satisfies the requirement that any finance charge rate be expressed as an APR. There is also File Now, Pay Later language: "Variable APR ranging from 15%-33%. For example, a 6-month $2,000 loan with an APR of 19% has a finance charge of $112.28..." which also states rates as APR. Thus the rule is triggered and fully complied with. There is no governing library text for APR phrasing, so axis B is not applicable. The strongest counter-argument would be that the example finance charge amount ($112.28) is given without repeating the APR acronym right next to it; however, the rate in that sentence (19%) is clearly identified as "APR," so the page still meets the rule's requirement.

findings.evidence_quote: File Now, Pay Later is available with a 3, 6, or 9 month loan term. Variable APR ranging from 15%-33%. For example, a 6-month $2,000 loan with an APR of 19% has a finance charge of $112.28 and 6 monthly installments of $352.05 each.
findings.location: File Now, Pay Later loan details and disclosures section
findings.reasoning: This separate product disclosure for File Now, Pay Later states finance charge rates as "Variable APR" with a range and an example "APR of 19%" tied to an explicit finance charge dollar amount. Because all percentages describing the finance cost are clearly labeled as APR, the requirement of R-02 is satisfied. As with the Refund Advance example, there is no specific library-verbiage standard to check, so axis B is not applicable. The counterargument that the phrase "finance charge of $112.28" appears without repeating APR is not persuasive because the example sentence already specifies that the 19% figure is an APR, and the rule only requires that any rate of finance charge be stated as an APR, which it is.

findings.location: TurboTax Online/Mobile offers & pricing – Refund Advance disclosures
findings.reasoning: A rate of finance charge is stated and it is explicitly expressed as an APR (0% APR). No library-governed wording applies, so Axis B is not applicable. A possible counter-reading is that fees are not APR, but the text directly states the rate as APR, satisfying the rule.

findings.location: TurboTax Online/Mobile offers & pricing – File Now, Pay Later disclosures
findings.reasoning: A finance charge rate is presented and clearly labeled as APR, meeting the requirement. No library entry governs phrasing, so Axis B is not applicable. A counter-reading might focus on the example finance charge dollar amount, but the presence of APR labeling for the rate resolves the requirement.
